question: What is homeostasis and how is it maintained?

options: The maintenance of a stable internal environment despite external change, achieved mainly by negative feedback loops (sensor → control centre → effector) that counteract deviations — regulating temperature, blood glucose, pH, osmolarity, etc.
Allowing the internal environment to vary freely with the surroundings (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (not supported by the evidence)
Maintained only by positive feedback that amplifies changes (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
A purely passive process requiring no regulation (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect)
passive.explanation: Homeostasis (Claude Bernard's 'constancy of the internal milieu', named by Walter Cannon) is the active maintenance of stable internal conditions essential for cells to function. The dominant mechanism is negative feedback: a sensor detects a variable, a control centre compares it to a set point, and effectors act to oppose any deviation, returning the variable toward the set point — like a thermostat. Examples: thermoregulation (sweating/shivering, vasodilation/constriction keep core temperature ~37 °C), blood-glucose control (insulin lowers, glucagon raises it), osmoregulation and blood pH (buffers, kidneys, lungs), blood pressure, and calcium balance. Positive feedback is rarer and used to drive processes to completion (childbirth contractions, blood clotting, the action-potential upstroke). Homeostatic systems often involve hormonal and neural control acting together. Failure of homeostasis underlies much disease (diabetes, fever, electrolyte disorders). The concept unifies physiology, explaining how multicellular organisms buffer their cells from a fluctuating world.

active.prompt: Match each organ system with its primary function:

pairs.definition: Heart + vessels transport O2, nutrients, hormones, and wastes; thermoregulation
Gas exchange — O2 in, CO2 out — across alveoli; helps regulate blood pH
Kidneys filter blood, regulate water/electrolytes/pH, excrete nitrogenous waste
Glands secrete hormones for slow, widespread regulation (metabolism, growth, reproduction)

active.explanation: Organ systems cooperate to maintain homeostasis: the circulatory system distributes materials, the respiratory and renal systems handle gas exchange and waste/fluid balance (and together regulate pH), the endocrine and nervous systems coordinate responses, and the digestive, immune, musculoskeletal, and integumentary systems contribute. Integration across systems is the hallmark of physiology.

question: How does the heart pump blood and what drives the circulation?

options: A double pump: the right side sends deoxygenated blood to the lungs (pulmonary circuit), the left side pumps oxygenated blood to the body (systemic circuit); rhythmic contraction is triggered by the SA node pacemaker, and a pressure gradient drives flow through arteries, capillaries, and veins
A single pump sending blood only to the lungs (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon)
Blood moves by diffusion alone with no pump (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship)
The heart contracts only under conscious control (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: The mammalian heart is a four-chambered double pump driving two circuits in series. The right atrium and ventricle receive deoxygenated blood from the body and pump it to the lungs (pulmonary circuit) for gas exchange; the left atrium and ventricle receive oxygenated blood from the lungs and pump it at high pressure to the whole body (systemic circuit). Valves (atrioventricular and semilunar) ensure one-way flow. Contraction is myogenic and rhythmic: the sinoatrial (SA) node spontaneously depolarises (the pacemaker, setting heart rate), the impulse spreads across the atria, is delayed at the AV node, then races through the bundle of His and Purkinje fibres to coordinate ventricular contraction — the electrical sequence recorded as the ECG (P-QRS-T). Cardiac output = heart rate × stroke volume, modulated by autonomic nerves (sympathetic speeds/strengthens, parasympathetic/vagus slows) and hormones, and by the Frank-Starling mechanism (greater filling → stronger contraction). Blood flows down a pressure gradient from arteries (high pressure, elastic) through arterioles (resistance/regulation) to capillaries (exchange) and back via veins (low pressure, aided by skeletal-muscle pumps and valves). This delivery of O2 and nutrients and removal of wastes sustains every tissue.

question: How does the endocrine system regulate the body with hormones?

options: Glands secrete hormones into the blood that act on distant target cells bearing specific receptors; control is often via feedback axes (e.g. hypothalamus → pituitary → target gland), giving slow, sustained, widespread regulation of metabolism, growth, stress, and reproduction
Hormones act instantly and only on adjacent cells (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this conflates cause and effect) (a common misconception)
The endocrine system uses electrical signals like nerves (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Hormones affect all cells equally regardless of receptors (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: The endocrine system provides slow, sustained, body-wide chemical regulation, complementing the fast, targeted nervous system. Endocrine glands release hormones into the bloodstream; these reach all tissues but act only on cells expressing the matching receptor — conferring specificity. Two broad classes: water-soluble hormones (peptides like insulin, and catecholamines) bind cell-surface receptors and act via second messengers (cAMP, Ca2+) for relatively rapid effects; lipid-soluble hormones (steroids like cortisol and sex hormones, and thyroid hormone) cross the membrane to bind intracellular receptors that regulate gene transcription, giving slower, longer-lasting effects. Much endocrine control runs through hierarchical axes with negative feedback: the hypothalamus signals the pituitary ('master gland'), which signals peripheral glands (thyroid, adrenal cortex, gonads), whose hormones feed back to damp the axis (e.g. the HPA stress axis: CRH → ACTH → cortisol, cortisol inhibiting upstream). Key examples: insulin/glucagon (glucose homeostasis), thyroid hormone (metabolic rate), cortisol (stress, metabolism), growth hormone, and the reproductive hormones. The nervous and endocrine systems are integrated (neuroendocrine), and endocrine disorders (diabetes, thyroid disease, etc.) are common and consequential.

question: How does the immune system defend the body?

options: Innate immunity gives fast, non-specific defence (barriers, phagocytes, inflammation, complement); adaptive immunity provides slower, specific, memory-forming responses — B cells make antibodies (humoral) and T cells kill infected cells or help coordinate (cell-mediated), distinguishing self from non-self
The body has no defence against pathogens (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception)
Only one type of white blood cell exists, with no memory (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence)
Immunity cannot distinguish the body's own cells from invaders (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: The immune system protects against pathogens and abnormal cells through two integrated arms. Innate immunity is the rapid, evolutionarily ancient, non-specific first line: physical/chemical barriers (skin, mucus, stomach acid), phagocytes (neutrophils, macrophages) that engulf microbes, natural killer cells, the complement cascade, inflammation, and fever, all triggered by recognising conserved microbial patterns (PAMPs) via pattern-recognition receptors. Adaptive (acquired) immunity is slower but specific and remembers: lymphocytes bearing uniquely rearranged receptors recognise specific antigens. B cells (humoral immunity) secrete antibodies that neutralise, opsonise, and flag pathogens for destruction; T cells (cell-mediated) include cytotoxic T cells that kill virus-infected or cancerous cells and helper T cells that coordinate the response by activating B cells and macrophages. Antigen-presenting cells (via MHC molecules) bridge innate and adaptive arms. Crucially, the system learns self-tolerance (failure causes autoimmunity) and forms immunological memory — long-lived memory cells enable a faster, stronger response on re-exposure, the principle exploited by vaccination. Dysregulation causes allergy (hypersensitivity), immunodeficiency, and autoimmune disease, while harnessing immunity (immunotherapy, checkpoint inhibitors) has transformed cancer treatment.

question: How do kidneys regulate water and solute balance?

options: Nephrons filter blood at the glomerulus, then reabsorb needed water/solutes and secrete wastes along the tubule; the loop of Henle builds a medullary osmotic gradient enabling concentrated urine, and hormones (ADH, aldosterone) fine-tune water and Na+ reabsorption to maintain fluid/electrolyte/pH balance
Kidneys simply store urine with no filtration (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship)
All filtered water and solutes are excreted unchanged (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon)
Hormones play no role in kidney function (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect)
passive.explanation: The kidneys maintain the volume, composition, and pH of body fluids and excrete nitrogenous waste, via ~1 million functional units (nephrons) each performing three processes. (1) Glomerular filtration: blood pressure forces a protein-free filtrate of plasma across the glomerular capillaries into Bowman's capsule (~180 L/day filtered). (2) Tubular reabsorption: as filtrate flows through the proximal tubule, loop of Henle, and distal tubule, the body reclaims most water, glucose, amino acids, and ions (so only ~1.5 L of urine results). The loop of Henle uses a countercurrent multiplier to build a steep osmotic gradient in the medulla, the key to producing urine more concentrated than blood and conserving water. (3) Tubular secretion: additional wastes, drugs, H+, and K+ are actively added to the filtrate. Hormonal control tunes the output to homeostatic needs: antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin) increases water reabsorption in the collecting duct (concentrating urine when dehydrated); aldosterone (from the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system) promotes Na+ (and thus water) reabsorption to regulate blood volume and pressure; and the kidneys regulate acid-base balance (reabsorbing bicarbonate, excreting H+) and produce hormones (erythropoietin, active vitamin D). This makes the kidney central to blood pressure, electrolyte, and pH homeostasis; its failure necessitates dialysis or transplant.

question: How does skeletal muscle contract (the sliding-filament mechanism)?

options: A motor-neuron signal releases Ca2+ from the sarcoplasmic reticulum; Ca2+ exposes actin binding sites, and myosin heads cyclically attach, pivot (power stroke), and detach using ATP, sliding actin past myosin to shorten the sarcomere — the cross-bridge cycle of the sliding-filament theory
Muscle filaments themselves shorten/coil up (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Contraction requires no calcium or ATP (this describes a different phenomenon) (this conflates cause and effect) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Muscle contracts by inflating with fluid (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception)
passive.explanation: Skeletal muscle contracts by the sliding-filament mechanism (Huxley): the thin (actin) and thick (myosin) filaments slide past each other, shortening each sarcomere (the repeating contractile unit) without the filaments themselves shortening. Excitation-contraction coupling links the nerve signal to force: a motor neuron releases acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction, depolarising the muscle fibre; the action potential travels down T-tubules and triggers Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. Ca2+ binds troponin, shifting tropomyosin to expose myosin-binding sites on actin. The cross-bridge cycle then runs: a myosin head (energised by prior ATP hydrolysis) binds actin, undergoes a conformational power stroke that pulls the thin filament inward, releases ADP+Pi, and — on binding a new ATP — detaches and re-cocks, repeating as long as Ca2+ and ATP are present. Relaxation follows when Ca2+ is pumped back into the SR (rigor mortis reflects ATP depletion locking the cross-bridges). Force grows with the number of recruited motor units and firing frequency (summation/tetanus), and depends on sarcomere length (the length-tension relationship). The same actomyosin principle, with variations, drives cardiac and (with different regulation) smooth muscle, and underlies all animal movement.
